summary: DHH argues that companies can operate with very few full-time managers by replacing traditional managerial supervision with well-designed asynchronous processes. He outlines five specific practices at 37signals: automated daily/weekly check-ins replacing stand-ups, 6-week Shape Up cycles limiting planning overhead, Hill Charts for project visibility, time-boxed cycles that enforce scope discipline, and peer mentoring for quality review. These processes allow 37signals to run with just one full-time engineering manager across all technical teams, while other managers (including DHH and Jason Fried) split their time between management and individual contribution. The goal is enabling individual contributors to operate as autonomous 'managers of one.'
key_points: Daily stand-ups and weekly 1-1s can be replaced with automated asynchronous check-in questions that keep everyone informed
Time-boxed 6-week cycles with fixed scope budgets eliminate the need for managers to constantly make trade-off decisions — teams self-manage within constraints
37signals operates with a single full-time engineering manager across all programming, operations, and design teams
Management functions like career guidance and feedback can be separated from routine supervision work
Peer mentoring distributes quality review across the team, with senior staff held accountable for their mentee's output
money_quotes: If you want to run a company that's light on full-time managers, you have to focus on managing processes before people.

The traditional paradigm of a reporting manager that's constantly following up with their reports, conducting daily stand-up meetings, weekly 1-1s, and all other forms of intensive supervision, needs to be (mostly) replaced with an asynchronous, self-managing paradigm instead.

Everyone working on a feature knows that they have to find a way to hammer the scope enough to ship within that maximum budget. This deputizes them to make the necessary trade-offs without the need of a manager.
These processes help us dramatically reduce the amount of managerial supervision needed at 37signals. They're the supporting pillars that make the Manager of One ideal actually hold up.
Jason and I are both moonlighting managers as well. Spending the majority of our time pushing the products forward with our direct work on design, programming, copywriting, or whatever.
These functions of management can be divorced from much of the other routine supervision work that fills the weeks of a manager under the traditional paradigm.
Get the processes right and the rest will follow.
key_insight: Well-designed asynchronous processes and time-boxed work cycles can replace most traditional managerial supervision, allowing individual contributors to operate autonomously while the company runs with minimal full-time managers.
